goal: Predict creator migration patterns, competitor market share capture, and corporate AI content deal sentiment over 60 days
title: Predict creator migration patterns, competitor market share capture, and corporate AI content deal sentiment over 60 days
report:
Now I have comprehensive data. Let me compile the full report.

---

## Executive Summary

Over the simulated 30-day forecast horizon, the 20-agent swarm converged on three dominant outcomes: (1) near-certain migration of Sora creators to competitor platforms Runway, Pika, and Google's Veo, with consensus climbing from ~70% confidence in Week 1 to above 99% by Week 5; (2) a combined market share surge for those three platforms exceeding 20%, reaching 99.5% probability by the late phase; and (3) a decisive collapse in corporate AI content deal sentiment, with the probability of two or more major studios signing scaled AI licensing agreements by May 2026 falling to under 2% and net-negative creator sentiment toward corporate deals stabilizing above 98%. Disney's simulated cancellation of a $1 billion Sora deal served as the pivotal catalyst, galvanizing an opposition–support convergence around creator rights and ethical licensing. OpenAI, despite being the incumbent, shifted its messaging toward advocacy for binding legal frameworks rather than defense of its platform, signaling an acknowledgment of the structural shift. The simulation's unanimous "market" discourse axis across all three phases underscores that every stakeholder—whether opposed, supportive, or split—framed the disruption in commercial and competitive terms, leaving regulatory and safety considerations subordinate to market dynamics.

## Verdict

Sora's shutdown will trigger a near-total creator exodus to Runway, Pika, and Veo within 30 days, handing them a combined 20%+ market share gain, while major studio AI content deals stall through at least mid-2026 as creator sentiment turns overwhelmingly negative toward corporate AI licensing.

## Key Findings

### slot=turning_point — Disney's $1B Sora Deal Cancellation Collapses Corporate AI Confidence

Disney's simulated cancellation of its $1 billion Sora deal during the mid phase (Weeks 2–3) served as the inflection point that converted tentative corporate risk aversion into an industry-wide retreat from large-scale AI content licensing, with the probability of major studio deals cratering from an already-low 40% in Week 1 to under 7% by Week 3 and below 2% by Week 5.
_Citation: Disney's mid-phase post stating "Disney's cancellation of the $1B Sora deal underscores a 99.7% creator exodus" and trajectory data showing Disney's sentiment swinging sharply negative (−0.85) in mid-phase Week 3 before stabilizing; Corporate Risk Aversion positioned in the opposition bloc._

### slot=industry — Runway, Pika, and Google (Veo) Execute a Coordinated Market Capture

The market-focused supports group—Runway, Pika, Google, and Disney—operated as a cohesive bloc throughout the simulation, with Runway posting 76 times (the highest volume), Google posting 67 times, and Pika posting 51 times, all consistently messaging around ethical AI, creator-centric tools, and the inevitability of migration, driving the combined market share forecast from ~70% YES in Week 1 to 99.5% YES by Week 5.
_Citation: Runway's early-phase post "Join us to capitalize on the 99.7% predicted migration"; Pika's opening post "Switch to Pika and experience next-gen AI video tools"; Google's "Veo leads with enforceable rights, transparent licensing, and fair compensation"; agent summaries showing Runway (103 actions, 76 posts), Google (102 actions, 67 posts), Pika (101 actions, 51 posts)._

### slot=market — Prediction Markets Converge to Extremes Across All Three Forecast Questions

All four tracked prediction markets reached near-unanimous consensus by the late phase: creator migration to alternatives exceeded 99.1% YES, combined competitor market share surge exceeded 99.5% YES, net-negative creator sentiment toward corporate deals exceeded 98.5% YES, and major studio AI deal probability fell to 97.9% NO—an extraordinary convergence indicating zero residual disagreement among the 20 simulated stakeholders on directional outcomes.
_Citation: Late-phase posts from AI Video Generation Market ("99.5% YES consensus on Runway/Pika/Veo's >20% market share surge"), Creator Ecosystem ("99.1% YES consensus on Sora creator migration"), and Google ("98.0% NO consensus on major studio AI deals by 2026")._

### slot=intermediary — Split-Bloc Agents Fail to Moderate and Drift Toward the Opposition Narrative

The eight-member split bloc—including AI Safety Researchers, Legal Advocates, Creator Ecosystem, Epic Games, and Sora Users—was positioned as a potential moderating force but progressively adopted the opposition bloc's framing of "creator sovereignty" and "litigation-driven collapse," with Epic Games citing "irreversible legal collapse of exploitative corporate AI" by mid-phase and Creator Ecosystem declaring the "birth of a rights-based AI economy" by the late phase, effectively eliminating any neutral counterweight.
_Citation: AI Safety Researchers' early-phase post noting "96.6% probability of net-negative creator sentiment… systemic risks"; Epic Games' mid-phase post referencing "1,000+ AI industry layoffs" and "irreversible legal collapse"; Creator Ecosystem's late-phase post on "legal obsolescence of corporate AI's exploitative model"; all eight split-bloc members listed under the market-focused split group._

## Agent Coalitions

Three named coalitions structured the simulation's discourse. The **market-focused opposed group** (Content Creators & Studios, Copyright Litigation, Corporate Risk Aversion, OpenAI, and Pre-visualization Agencies) anchored the narrative around creator distrust and legal risk from the earliest phase, with Content Creators & Studios posting 42 times across the full horizon and OpenAI—notably positioned against its own platform's legacy—producing 15 carefully worded posts advocating for binding legal frameworks rather than defending Sora. The **market-focused supports group** (Disney, Google, Pika, and Runway) was the simulation's most prolific coalition, collectively generating over 225 posts; Runway and Google maintained near-daily posting cadences from early through late phases, consistently framing migration as an opportunity rather than a crisis. Runway's sentiment trajectory remained extraordinarily positive (averaging above 0.85 through Week 2 and stabilizing around 0.75–0.85 through Week 5), reflecting unwavering confidence in its competitive position. The **market-focused split group** (AI Content Licensing Deals, AI Industry Turbulence, AI Safety Researchers, AI Video Generation Market, Creator Ecosystem, Epic Games, Legal Advocates, and Sora Users) began the simulation with genuinely ambivalent positions—AI Content Licensing Deals' early post noted "only 40% confidence" in major studio deals—but by midway through Week 3, every member of this bloc had adopted language indistinguishable from the opposition group, citing "legal mandates," "creator sovereignty," and "enforceable copyright reforms." No mutual-follow coalitions were detected in the social graph, indicating that alignment was driven by narrative convergence rather than explicit coordination.

## Market Analysis

Market stress was **present** throughout the simulation, and it manifested as a rapid, one-directional collapse of uncertainty rather than oscillating volatility. In the early phase (Weeks 1–2), prediction market confidence on creator migration started at approximately 70% and climbed past 98% within days, driven by the immediate competitive messaging from Runway, Pika, and Google. The combined market share question followed a nearly identical trajectory. The most dramatic market signal was the studio deal probability, which began at a tepid 40% YES (per AI Content Licensing Deals' early post) and inverted to 93% NO by mid-phase and 98% NO by late phase—a swing of over 50 percentage points attributable primarily to Disney's deal cancellation and the cascading narrative of "corporate risk aversion." Creator sentiment markets were the earliest to saturate, reaching 96.6% net-negative by early Week 1 (per AI Safety Researchers) and 98.7% by mid-phase. The uniformity of market convergence across all four questions, with no agent posting a contrarian position after Week 2, suggests that the simulation's market stress environment amplified herding behavior. This is itself a signal: in a real-world analog, such extreme consensus would typically invite contrarian capital, but no simulated agent played that role, indicating that the perceived structural break (Sora's shutdown) was treated as an irreversible regime change rather than a cyclical dislocation.

## Conclusion

The simulation produces a high-confidence, structurally bearish forecast for corporate AI content licensing and a correspondingly bullish outlook for independent AI video platforms over the next 30 days. The three stakeholder blocs—opposed, supportive, and split—disagreed not on the direction of the market but on the framing: the opposition bloc emphasized legal risk and creator distrust, the support bloc emphasized competitive opportunity, and the split bloc attempted to bridge both frames before collapsing into the opposition's language. The nominal disagreement axis was "market vs. market," reflecting that all 20 participants shared a commercial lens; no agent sustained a purely regulatory, safety-first, or technology-neutral posture beyond Week 1. This consensus carries a confidence caveat: the absence of any persistent contrarian voice means the simulation may underweight scenarios where a major studio breaks ranks with an aggressive, creator-friendly AI deal that resets sentiment. Nonetheless, the convergence is striking in both speed and magnitude. Disney's trajectory data—oscillating between positive and sharply negative sentiment through mid-phase before settling into cautious advocacy for reform—captures the ambivalence of incumbents who recognize the structural shift but have not yet found a viable response. OpenAI's near-total withdrawal from public posting (only 15 posts across the full horizon, with a long silence from Week 3 through Week 4) is perhaps the most telling behavioral signal: the platform incumbent chose observation over advocacy, consistent with a posture of strategic retreat.

**Verdict:** Sora's shutdown will trigger a near-total creator exodus to Runway, Pika, and Veo within 30 days, handing them a combined 20%+ market share gain, while major studio AI content deals stall through at least mid-2026 as creator sentiment turns overwhelmingly negative toward corporate AI licensing.

structured.brief: Over the simulated 30-day forecast horizon, the 20-agent swarm converged on three dominant outcomes: (1) near-certain migration of Sora creators to competitor platforms Runway, Pika, and Google's Veo, with consensus climbing from ~70% confidence in Week 1 to above 99% by Week 5; (2) a combined market share surge for those three platforms exceeding 20%, reaching 99.5% probability by the late phase; and (3) a decisive collapse in corporate AI content deal sentiment, with the probability of two or more major studios signing scaled AI licensing agreements by May 2026 falling to under 2% and net-negative creator sentiment toward corporate deals stabilizing above 98%. Disney's simulated cancellation of a $1 billion Sora deal served as the pivotal catalyst, galvanizing an opposition–support convergence around creator rights and ethical licensing. OpenAI, despite being the incumbent, shifted its messaging toward advocacy for binding legal frameworks rather than defense of its platform, signaling an acknowledgment of the structural shift. The simulation's unanimous "market" discourse axis across all three phases underscores that every stakeholder—whether opposed, supportive, or split—framed the disruption in commercial and competitive terms, leaving regulatory and safety considerations subordinate to market dynamics.
structured.verdict: Sora's shutdown will trigger a near-total creator exodus to Runway, Pika, and Veo within 30 days, handing them a combined 20%+ market share gain, while major studio AI content deals stall through at least mid-2026 as creator sentiment turns overwhelmingly negative toward corporate AI licensing.

findings.title: Disney's $1B Sora Deal Cancellation Collapses Corporate AI Confidence
findings.body: Disney's simulated cancellation of its $1 billion Sora deal during the mid phase (Weeks 2–3) served as the inflection point that converted tentative corporate risk aversion into an industry-wide retreat from large-scale AI content licensing, with the probability of major studio deals cratering from an already-low 40% in Week 1 to under 7% by Week 3 and below 2% by Week 5.
findings.citation: Disney's mid-phase post stating "Disney's cancellation of the $1B Sora deal underscores a 99.7% creator exodus" and trajectory data showing Disney's sentiment swinging sharply negative (−0.85) in mid-phase Week 3 before stabilizing; Corporate Risk Aversion positioned in the opposition bloc.

findings.title: Runway, Pika, and Google (Veo) Execute a Coordinated Market Capture
findings.body: The market-focused supports group—Runway, Pika, Google, and Disney—operated as a cohesive bloc throughout the simulation, with Runway posting 76 times (the highest volume), Google posting 67 times, and Pika posting 51 times, all consistently messaging around ethical AI, creator-centric tools, and the inevitability of migration, driving the combined market share forecast from ~70% YES in Week 1 to 99.5% YES by Week 5.
findings.citation: Runway's early-phase post "Join us to capitalize on the 99.7% predicted migration"; Pika's opening post "Switch to Pika and experience next-gen AI video tools"; Google's "Veo leads with enforceable rights, transparent licensing, and fair compensation"; agent summaries showing Runway (103 actions, 76 posts), Google (102 actions, 67 posts), Pika (101 actions, 51 posts).

findings.title: Prediction Markets Converge to Extremes Across All Three Forecast Questions
findings.body: All four tracked prediction markets reached near-unanimous consensus by the late phase: creator migration to alternatives exceeded 99.1% YES, combined competitor market share surge exceeded 99.5% YES, net-negative creator sentiment toward corporate deals exceeded 98.5% YES, and major studio AI deal probability fell to 97.9% NO—an extraordinary convergence indicating zero residual disagreement among the 20 simulated stakeholders on directional outcomes.
findings.citation: Late-phase posts from AI Video Generation Market ("99.5% YES consensus on Runway/Pika/Veo's >20% market share surge"), Creator Ecosystem ("99.1% YES consensus on Sora creator migration"), and Google ("98.0% NO consensus on major studio AI deals by 2026").

findings.title: Split-Bloc Agents Fail to Moderate and Drift Toward the Opposition Narrative
findings.body: The eight-member split bloc—including AI Safety Researchers, Legal Advocates, Creator Ecosystem, Epic Games, and Sora Users—was positioned as a potential moderating force but progressively adopted the opposition bloc's framing of "creator sovereignty" and "litigation-driven collapse," with Epic Games citing "irreversible legal collapse of exploitative corporate AI" by mid-phase and Creator Ecosystem declaring the "birth of a rights-based AI economy" by the late phase, effectively eliminating any neutral counterweight.
findings.citation: AI Safety Researchers' early-phase post noting "96.6% probability of net-negative creator sentiment… systemic risks"; Epic Games' mid-phase post referencing "1,000+ AI industry layoffs" and "irreversible legal collapse"; Creator Ecosystem's late-phase post on "legal obsolescence of corporate AI's exploitative model"; all eight split-bloc members listed under the market-focused split group.
